flashcards.back: If p is true, then q must be true. It is false only when p is true and q is false; true otherwise.

flashcards.front: What is the contrapositive of (p → q)?
flashcards.back: The contrapositive is (¬q → ¬q). It is logically equivalent to the original implication.

flashcards.front: What is the converse of (p → q)?
flashcards.back: The converse is (q → p). It is NOT logically equivalent to the original implication.

flashcards.front: What is the inverse of (p → q)?
flashcards.back: The inverse is (¬p → ¬q). It is logically equivalent to the converse.

flashcards.front: What is Modus Ponens (Law of Detachment)?
flashcards.back: From (p → q) and p, conclude q. Form: [(p → q) ∧ p] ⇒ q
From (p → q) and ¬q, conclude ¬p. Form: [(p → q) ∧ ¬q] ⇒ ¬p
From (p → q) and (q → r), conclude (p → r). Form: [(p → q) ∧ (q → r)] ⇒ (p → r)
From (p ∨ q) and ¬p, conclude q. Form: [(p ∨ q) ∧ ¬p] ⇒ q

flashcards.front: What is the Addition Rule (Disjunction Introduction)?
flashcards.back: From p, conclude (p ∨ q). Form: p ⇒ (p ∨ q)

flashcards.front: What is the Simplification Rule (Conjunction Elimination)?
flashcards.back: From (p ∧ q), conclude p. Form: (p ∧ q) ⇒ p
From p and q, conclude (p ∧ q). Form: [p ∧ q] ⇒ (p ∧ q)

flashcards.front: What is a valid argument in logic?
flashcards.back: An argument is valid if whenever all premises are true, the conclusion must also be true. The truth of conclusion follows necessarily from premises.
A sound argument is a valid argument where all premises are actually true in the real world.
From (p ∨ q) and (¬q ∨ r), conclude (p ∨ r). Form: [(p ∨ q) ∧ (¬q ∨ r)] ⇒ (p ∨ r)
